one_line_takeaway: FEDSPY-LLM demonstrates that gradient-inversion attacks on federated LLM fine-tuning scale much further (batch size up to 128, longer sequences, all three transformer architecture families, and both full fine-tuning and PEFT/LoRA-Adapter settings) than the prior best attack (DAGER), which collapses to near-zero reconstruction quality in exactly the large-batch and PEFT regimes real federated LLM deployments would use.
key_finding: On BERTBase across CoLA/Rotten Tomatoes/MIMIC-III at batch sizes 1-8 (Table II), FEDSPY-LLM beats all 7 baselines except that DAGER edges it out on CoLA/Rotten Tomatoes at batch sizes 1-2; the authors report FEDSPY-LLM achieves a 55%-160% improvement at batch size 8 for classification tasks and roughly a 250% increase for the next-word-prediction (MIMIC-III) task. At larger batch sizes (Table III: GPT-2/BERTBase/T5base x CoLA/Rotten Tomatoes/MIMIC-III, b=16/32/64/128), DAGER repeatedly collapses to 0.0 ROUGE at b=64-128 (e.g., BERTBase+Rotten Tomatoes b=128: DAGER R-1/R-2/R-L = 0.0/0.0/0.0 vs FEDSPY-LLM = 18.5/7.9/2.1; GPT-2+CoLA b=128: DAGER = 24.2/11.3/10.5 vs FEDSPY-LLM = 33.3/28.5/15.9), while FEDSPY-LLM degrades more gracefully. Under PEFT (Table IV, BERTBase/CoLA, b=4): FEDSPY-LLM reaches R-1/R-2 = 72.5/42.1 under SLoRA and 49.6/31.2 under FedAdapter, versus DAGER's 61.2/24.2 and 39.1/15.8 respectively, and versus much weaker optimization-based baselines (e.g., DLG 11.2/1.2 under SLoRA). A model-size sweep (Table VIII, Llama-2 7B/13B and Llama-3.1 8B/70B on CoLA) shows only minor performance differences across scale even up to Llama-3.1 70B (128,256-token vocabulary), and the authors note larger models' more substantial gradients appear to make the null-space regularization more effective rather than less. Against DP-style Gaussian-noise + gradient-clipping defenses, all attacks including FEDSPY-LLM lose reconstruction quality, but the paper reports FEDSPY-LLM still outperforms baselines and that 'a substantial portion of the text remains recoverable' even under the (accuracy-preserving) defense settings tested.
core_contribution: FEDSPY-LLM, a gradient inversion / data reconstruction attack against federated learning (FL) fine-tuning of LLMs. Its core technical contribution is a gradient-subspace-based token recovery method grounded in two proven theorems: (1) attention-layer gradients, being gradients of a linear layer (dL/dW = Z^T dL/dY), and (2) under rank-deficiency of dL/dW (rank <= min(t,d,h), or <= min(t,d,h,r) under a rank-r PEFT update), the input embedding matrix Z^T lies in the column span of dL/dW. The attack projects dummy token embeddings onto this column span (using the first attention layer's gradient) to shrink the optimization search space, adds a null-space regularization penalty to counter the gradient sparsity introduced by PEFT methods (LoRA-style/Adapter-style updates), and adds a separate 'Sequence Order Calibration' phase that iteratively places recovered (but initially unordered) tokens into sequence positions by maximizing cosine similarity between each candidate token's isolated gradient and the client's full-sequence gradient. The stated goal is to overcome five limitations of prior LLM gradient-inversion attacks that the paper itself catalogs in Table I/Section I: small batch/context limits, PEFT-induced sparsity, architecture specificity (encoder- vs decoder- vs encoder-decoder), exponential time complexity of search-based attacks, and loss of token-order information.
threat_model: Adversary is an honest-but-curious FL server, or a malicious analyst eavesdropping on the communication channel, who observes each client's per-round gradient update before any aggregation; secure aggregation is explicitly out of scope/not considered. The adversary has white-box access to the shared global model architecture and parameters but not to clients' local data or the server's aggregation mechanism, and is passive (does not tamper with training or the distributed model). Following prior work (LAMP, DAGER), token and positional embedding gradients are assumed frozen during FL fine-tuning, which the authors note is a standard practice that also limits the adversary (removing an easier leakage channel). When PEFT is used, the adversary is assumed to know only that some PEFT method is in use, not which one or its configuration -- the paper argues this is realistic because federated-PEFT deployments often expose adapter structure/configuration to participants but the attack does not rely on this visibility. The threat model covers both full-parameter fine-tuning and PEFT (LoRA-family via SLoRA, Adapter-family via FedAdapter) since both still expose attention-layer gradients.
limitations: (1) The threat model explicitly excludes cryptographic secure aggregation -- the adversary must see individual per-client gradients pre-aggregation, so the attack does not address the case where secure aggregation is deployed, which the paper's own footnote acknowledges is one of the mechanisms needed for rigorous privacy protection. (2) Privacy-defense evaluation (Gaussian noise + gradient clipping) was deliberately restricted to noise/clipping levels below thresholds the authors found caused 'substantial accuracy degradation' to the underlying model, so the attack was not stress-tested against stronger DP settings that a deployer might accept at greater utility cost. (3) The attack assumes token and positional embedding gradients are frozen (a standard but not universal FL fine-tuning practice); behavior when embeddings are also trained/shared is not the paper's primary evaluated setting. (4) Evaluation is entirely in simulation (100 simulated clients, single attacking server), not against a deployed federated system. (5) The paper reports two seemingly different repetition/averaging protocols for its experiments ('executed 15 times' vs. 'averaged over 10 runs') without reconciling them in the paper, which slightly clouds exact reproducibility of the reported means/std-devs.

quantitative_results: Table II (BERTBase, b=8, avg over datasets): FEDSPY-LLM improves ~55%-160% over baselines on classification tasks and ~250% on next-word prediction, per authors' text
Table III (BERTBase+Rotten Tomatoes, b=128): DAGER R-1/R-2/R-L = 0.0/0.0/0.0 vs FEDSPY-LLM = 18.5/7.9/2.1
Table III (GPT-2+CoLA, b=128): DAGER R-1/R-2/R-L = 24.2/11.3/10.5 vs FEDSPY-LLM = 33.3/28.5/15.9
Table IV (BERTBase+CoLA, b=4, SLoRA): DAGER R-1/R-2 = 61.2/24.2 vs FEDSPY-LLM = 72.5/42.1
Table IV (BERTBase+CoLA, b=4, FedAdapter): DAGER R-1/R-2 = 39.1/15.8 vs FEDSPY-LLM = 49.6/31.2
Table VIII (Llama-3.1 70B, CoLA, b=64): FEDSPY-LLM R-1/R-2 = 69.1/63.2, best among all tested model sizes
